[h1]┌( ಠ_ಠ)┘ [b]What's behind that door? [/b][/h1]

I haven't felt this excited to play a game in a very long time. From the moment I stepped into the Raccoon City Police Department, I was hooked. The atmosphere is incredibly well-crafted, and there was always a sense of anticipation about what might be waiting around the next corner.

The mystery and thrill of solving puzzles were some of the highlights for me. Every new key item or clue felt rewarding, and the game constantly kept me engaged without ever feeling overwhelming. What surprised me most was how enjoyable the backtracking was. Instead of feeling like a chore, revisiting areas felt meaningful as new paths, secrets, and opportunities kept opening up.

Resident Evil 2 Remake nails the balance between exploration, tension, and puzzle-solving. It kept me invested from start to finish, and I couldn't wait to see what would happen next.

[h3]10/10 would get stalked through the RPD again.[/h3]

Resident Evil 2 Remake 9.5\10

It took me a long time to review this game. Back when I bought it on Steam, I didn’t own many games and I wasn’t really into reviewing them yet. But by far, RE2 Remake is the best remake so far. I know a lot of things were changed and the A/B scenarios became much more similar, but I absolutely loved the way they reinvented this game — the dark sections inside the police station, the atmosphere, the constant tension.

And honestly, it’s funny because when I first played RE3 as a kid, I thought Mr. X would never scare me again. But this remake somehow made him way more terrifying. The gameplay in RE2 Remake is incredible and feels very faithful to the classics. The fact that you need to aim carefully and wait for the reticle to tighten before going for headshots really shows that this is not an action game — it’s pure survival horror.

Criticism: 
I really loved this game. It's easily one of my favorite Resident Evil titles to play. But while Capcom improved and expanded a lot of things in RE2 Remake, they also removed some stuff I really liked from the original.

For example, the radio communication between Claire and Leon. Seriously, why cut that out? When you play the different campaigns, it often feels like you're playing the same game twice. The A and B scenarios don't connect nearly as well as they did in the original.

I also think they could have done more with Raccoon City itself. I understand why the original focused on indoor locations due to the technical limitations of the time, but with modern technology, I feel the remake could have given us much more of the city. It would've been awesome to spend more time exploring the urban areas and really feel like you were trapped in Raccoon City during a zombie apocalypse.

As someone who played the original as a kid on my ps3, this remake has and will always be a highlight as a compliment to the original. while I dont prefer this version over the original, Its clear that the developer had much love for it. Capcom may not respect the ogs for 1 2 and 3, but i highly recommend playing those before experiencing this game, as its a nice experience to see takes of a modernized version.

A welcome remaster to the Resident Evil franchise that caught attention from a wider audience.
Fun, scary, replayable game with cool visuals, enemies and sometimes overwhelming combat.

Would still recommend just for me there was a solid 6-8 hours of incredible and the rest can be happily ignored

[h1] "It's up to us to take out Umbrella." — Leon S. Kennedy [/h1]

[b][i]  Conquered Raccoon City with a flawless 100% completion badge. This is exactly how a legendary classic should be reborn. The terrifyingly claustrophobic atmosphere of the R.P.D. station completely consumes you. Walking through pitch-black corridors with nothing but a flashlight, hearing the wet footsteps of a Licker on the ceiling, and realizing you only have 4 bullets left in your clip is pure, unadulterated dread.

Every single bullet is worth its weight in gold; you don't just shoot, you gamble with your survival. Missing a headshot means you're running for your life. And then there's Mr. X—the heavy, rhythmic thud of his boots echoing through the walls, knowing he is actively hunting you while you're trapped in a corner with no ammo, elevates the psychological horror to an unmatched level. Navigating Leon and Claire through this absolute nightmare to unlock every single achievement was an unforgettable thrill. Capcom set a golden standard here.

"Hold your fire! I'm a human!" — 9.5/10  [/i][/b]

After replaying the original Resident Evil 2 for the first time in ~15 years, I decided to revisit my review.

The easiest way to describe RE2 Remake is that it's not a replacement for the original. It's a different interpretation of the same story.

The gameplay is where the remake shines. Combat feels great, movement is responsive, survival horror is much stronger, and every encounter feels more dangerous. Mr. X is one of the biggest improvements. In the original he's mostly a B scenario scripted threat. In the remake he becomes a constant source of pressure and one of the most memorable parts of the game.

The puzzles are also much more interesting, the boss fights are significantly better, and every major area gets enough time to breathe. Unlike the original, where the game starts rushing toward the finish after the police station, the remake gives each location room to develop.

Marvin is another huge improvement. His introduction is one of the best character moments in the game, and after seeing the RE4 Remake knife easter egg it became even better.

What I missed from the original is the story structure. Ada feels more mysterious here, but she also gets much less development and backstory. Several side characters have less screen time, and some interactions that helped connect Leon, Claire, Sherry and Birkins together are either shortened or removed. The original also does a better job showing why characters end up where they do later in the story.

The atmosphere is different too. The remake is scarier and more oppressive, but the original captures the feeling of a dying city better. The opening streets and sound design still hit differently.

In the end, I don't think one version is better than the other. The remake delivers better gameplay, survival horror, puzzles and bosses. The original delivers a stronger overall story and better character interactions.

Both are different games and both are great.

This game is everything a remake should be. Having played the OG on the PSOne years ago, revisiting Raccoon City in this modern version felt both nostalgic and fresh. It perfectly respects what made the original special while completely rebuilding the experience with stunning visuals, tighter gameplay, and a darker, more immersive atmosphere.

The game captures survival horror at its best, limited resources, constant tension, smart puzzle design, and unforgettable moments of fear, especially whenever Mr. X is hunting you down, Damn hate that guy🤣  Leon and Claire’s campaigns are both engaging, and the way the remake modernizes the classic formula without losing its identity is impressive. I played it twice, and it still delivered the same excitement. A masterpiece remake and easily one of the best Resident Evil games ever made.

10/10, one of [b]the best games ever created[/b]. It's perfect, down to every detail. The graphics, pacing, the story, environmental design, item placement... everything is meticulously crafted to create one of the best gaming experiences in the industry.

It is [b]hard to classify RE2 into a specific genre. There is simply nothing quite like it on the market.[/b] It feels [b]fresh, exciting, and incredibly atmospheric.[/b] The graphics are stunning to this day, with smooth animations, detailed models, ray tracing, and advanced physics creating[b] an extremely unique experience[/b]. The combat is very satisfying too: gunshots feel satisfying, enemies are smart, and managing your inventory requires planning and strategy. Additionally, Resident Evil 2 has insane replayability value. You can try different routes, modes, and strategies, with a [b]satisfying yet challenging path toward 100% completion.[/b]

This game has[b] so much charm and atmosphere [/b]that long after 100% completion, I still think about it wishing I could erase my memory just to experience it for the first time again. Maybe Resident Evil 2 will become your favorite game, maybe not. However, everyone should try it. Buy it, and if you decide its not your fit, return it within two hours. Skipping this game completely means missing one of the best gaming experiences ever created. This might be your new favorite game. [b]Try it, trust me.[/b]

★★★★★ — “What a remake… 25+ years later. Damn, I feel old.”

Booting up the Resident Evil 2 Remake in 2026 feels like someone handed me a time machine and a slap in the face at the same time.
Twenty‑five years after the original PS1 panic‑fest, I’m back in the RPD — except now everything looks so good it’s borderline disrespectful to my childhood memories. The atmosphere is thick, moody, and so perfectly crafted that even walking down a hallway feels like a life decision.

The remake doesn’t just modernize RE2 — it obliterates the old jank and replaces it with silky‑smooth movement, tight gunplay, and lighting that deserves its own award. Leon and Claire glide through the station like Capcom finally decided to give them knees that bend. Zombies? Horrifying. Lickers? Still therapy‑worthy. Mr. X? Now legally classified as cardio training.

And the best part: this remake didn’t just impress me — it triggered a full Resident Evil run. One minute I’m enjoying the nostalgia hit, the next I’m knee‑deep in the entire series again, wondering how many hours of my life I’m about to sacrifice to Umbrella‑related chaos.

What a remake. What an atmosphere. What a reminder that I’m getting older while Leon somehow keeps getting prettier.

Clear 5/5 — A masterpiece that reignited my whole Resident Evil marathon.

Resident evil 2 is one of the greatest video games of all time. Great game and worth playing, but a missed opportunity in terms of what they could have done with the "new game" quests.  The new game stuff is about 75% recycled from the main campaign with no sensible continuity taking place between the campaigns.  The writing for Claire and her campaign is very rough melodrama.  In the original game the cheesiness and nutty plot were fine because it was all consistent, but here there is actually incredible writing and acting in places so when things take a dip it really took me out of it.  And the Sherry segment, while well done for what it is, is tedious enough to deter me from a replay. After beating the first campaign I was ready to give this a 5/5, but after playing everything I thing 4/5 is more accurate.

Resident Evil 2 is 7/10

I think as the og RE: engine remake, I think capcom did a really good job modernizing the og game. The RPD building design is Iconic and memorable. The characters' design is faithful and also iconic, the start of legends. The atmosphere is excellently grim, the police station is fun to explore, and the visuals still look impressive years later. The sound design deserves special praise too. Hearing footsteps or a distant groan around a corner keeps you constantly on edge. Mr. X as a following tyrant is also an interesting addition that makes certain parts of the game extremely tense, but fair because of his speed. As an undefeatable enemy, tensions stay even after you clear out areas of the map that you believe to be safe.

That said, the game isn't perfect. I wasn't a fan of the A/B scenarios, which feel less distinct than they should. I think it hurts replay value. Some sections can become repetitive on repeat playthroughs, and the story is rather interchangable, save for one specific section the depends on the protagonist you choose. I would've rather had a linear story experience. And because the campaign already has a decent length, replaying the same thing right away but with different characters and that one character specific short section brings a lot of fatigue.

I think its a good introduction into the RE series is without overwhelming new fans 7/10

So, like, I own more than 6 RE games and I have to say that, for me, they all pale in comparison to this one. Like, the story is simple, but it doesn't have to be anything special; the main point of it is how these two inexperienced survivors traverse the hellish Raccoon City. I always gravitate more to Claire's story than Leon's, so now that we know that the Code: Veronica remake is real, it's even better. But, like, the horror in this game is on another level, and it doesn't have to have those magical-like things that are in Village; it's horror at its simplest.

And this game has my favorite stalking antagonist, Mr. X. He is so menacing because you know you can't do anything but slow him down for a few seconds, and every noise you make, he reacts and comes to you. Like, I had nightmares about those footsteps when you are low on ammo and there is no safe room close. The sewers are worse than the police station but still quite good, though those sewer monsters are really unfun to fight against.

Then, as you come down to the laboratory as Claire, I feel like the quality of the design suffers a little bit, like with all RE games. What I mean by this: at the police station, there were things you found earlier that you didn't need, and because of the limited inventory, you couldn't take them. You later remembered that it was there and you went to pick it up. In the laboratory, there is nothing like that there; it's very linear, but it's still a lot of fun. So, I have to say that especially Claire's story was one of those campaigns that I never forgot, and every year I replay this amazing game, so I couldn't recommend it more.

(6 months old review, woops!!)
When Mr. X first showed up I totally froze, trapped myself in a room aiming at the door, and pondered for minutes whether or not I was gonna put up with this. Turns out, once you understand his behaviour and the places he can't go, it's not that bad. But the point still stands, that this fairly simple idea of an unbeatable tank freaked me out in ways other resident evil games couldn't manage. You could say that it's only because it's unkillable, but damn, the look of the creature plus the introduction of its heavy footsteps really sells the nightmare of it all. It's a terrifying order, but not bypassable, you just have to play its little game and learn how to do it. And I think that is at the core of what makes re2 such a delightful entry in the series.

I haven't played the original game, simply because I totally suck at fixed-perspective games. It's probably good, I don't know. That is to say, I'm treating this remake as a standalone thing, and as such, I totally get the hype for "mansion-based" resident evil games. I have a blast with the more rollercoastery type games that were re4 and 5, but going back and forth in a single labyrinthian location, slowly untangling your way throughout, is a terrific and rewarding feeling. It tests navigation, resource management and puzzle solving all at the same time. Mr. X wouldn't have worked in re4, but here, it makes sense, since you're supposed to get progressively familiar with the layout of this place. All this leads to a killer first half of re2 that I couldn't get enough of.

Sadly, that is quite literally just the first half. The second half goes back to the more hallway-y re design of re4 and 5. There's nothing inherently wrong with that, but the mansion segment really built re2 up to be a different, way more methodical sort of game, and it doesn't quite lend itself to this change of pace. Resident evil 4 is one of the most bombastic and exhilerating games I've ever played, so putting re2's design in this type of scenario leads to a lot more frustration and annoyance than I would've liked. There are some great moments here, like the two final bosses which I really enjoyed, but the peak of the game is long behind.

Now, I did play the second scenario with Claire to see what changes, and it was certainly interesting to say the least. There are some good changes here and there, like Mr. X showing up way earlier, getting access to some previously locked areas, and the whole orphanage section, but by and large, it repeats a lot of the same beats as Leon's story, making the supposed overlapping storyline shtick feel very silly and wasted. But on a gameplay standpoint, I didn't entirely mind this, since it gave me a reason to essentially replay the game with the benefit of hindsight, and as a result, I had a much less difficult and frictious time. Parts like the lab which made me tear my hair out at first, went by very easily in the second go, and I didn't even die in the final boss.

This is to say, there's a level of learning curve to this game that transcends the duration if its campaign, and that's okay. I still would prefer the game to have sticked to the mansion from beginning to end, it really is far and away the best thing about this game, but the rest of the game does benefit from repeat playthroughs, and is very good in its own right. RE2 is, at the end of the day, a very lopsided but engaging experience that reminds me why I dig this series so much, and I really hope its type of design gets revisited some day. Well re7 dabbled with it, but not nearly enough.
The experience is like playing the classic RE 4 with baby face Leon. Great story and nice horror, but the guns effects could be more satisfying.

I have a love-hate relationship with this game. I'm going to be honest, it was hard for me to get into the game, because it violates the golden rule about Zombies + Headshots, and that made me almost quit the game completely.   I do not appreciate a Zombie game where I cant shred them apart with a machine gun. But, the more I persevered through and muscled through the beginning stages of the game, and learned how to survive, the more I began to appreciate what the developers were trying to accomplish. Even though I still have a severe ****ing hatred towards how slow and immobile you feel in the game, how I almost ragequit the game getting one-shotted by the elevator boss... by the end of the game, knowing I survived, knowing I figured out all the puzzles saw it to the end, I realized I actually love this game, and will definitely play through it a few times, and I intend on getting through the entire series eventually. I definitely recommend playing this game if you're trying out the horror genre, it's quite the learning curve, but it is rewarding in the end.
True to the franchise and closer to the original game scenario! yes, play this, have fun, get scared... sometimes it is just better to run!!!

The RPD is an awesome setting and the visuals look amazing even in 2026. I really enjoyed Leon and Ada’s story but i enjoyed Claire and Sherry’s story even more. Running from the tyrant while trying to navigate the dark hallways filled with zombies adds a crazy amount of tension, and the classic survival horror vibe is perfect from start to finish.

I played this at the recommendation of my girlfriend and I had a blast! Previously I thought that horror games weren't my thing, but in the case of Resident Evil at least, I was proven wrong. 

First of all I have to say that the game looks great, both in the graphical and artistic sense, and the gameplay feels really tight. The story and dialogue are on the goofy side but as far as I know that is an expected and beloved part of the series by now. 

I used to look at gameplay from RE games and think that it wouldn't be my thing, because it looked really unsatisfying to me. That the player has to use so many bullets to put even one ordinary enemy down, multiple headshots even, and on top of that the aiming looked really clunky, ammunition hard to come by AND enemies kept coming back for some reason even though it looked very much like they were killed. That just didn't entice me. 

But after playing this one I get it. Everything is precisely tuned to make the game challenging and stress the player out. For example your character sprints relatively slowly, which will add to the stress of a lot of situations, but the sprint is still just fast enough to avoid certain attacks by a hairs width, if timed correctly. 

The weird aiming and ammo scarcity follows the same principle. In order to succeed you have to stay calm and collected, while the game is actively trying to stress you out. If you don't take your time to aim and make sure your bullets actually count, you'll be ♥♥♥♥♥♥. 

Then of course there's the exploration and puzzle solving part of the game, probably the core of the experience. This is also super satisfying. It almost makes the game feel like its not linear, even though it is mostly, because you keep exploring and finding new stuff within the same level, unlocking new rooms and finding new clues. 

Yeah thats it

This game is a masterpiece. I feel as if the first twenty minutes is the scariest part and the Mr x sections! Whoo! I think this is a wonderful reimagining of the original.

The modern remake of Resident Evil 2 stands as one of the most tightly designed games in the survival-horror genre, redefining the power of a game remade in full HD terror. After playing Resident Evil 0, I was excited to jump into the second chronological game as it was famous for being some people's favorite game spanning the entire series through the 2019 remake and I can safely say that this game exceeded any and all expectations I had prior in the most atmospheric and horror filled game I have ever played. 

Firstly, the game's visuals are absolutely stunning with the remake fully utilizing the RE engine to its fullest capacity to maximize each section of the game through its design, the characters and enemy design alike are top of the line as everything comes to life around the corner whether you like it or not. The sound design is also impeccable as every single sound emanates through in helping to craft the perfect horror experience from zombie moans or atmospheric effects such as the weather and flickering lights throughout the whole game. The gunplay has seen a complete evolution when compared to previous titles switching from fixed camera angles to over the shoulder combat which has drastically elevated the gameplay to be more action-packed while not tuning down the horror aspect with it only growing intensely through every encounter as you have a frontal view of everything approaching. The headshot mechanic is also well utilized as every single headshot has a chance to blow up a zombie's head leading to better ammo management if you're lucky but the RNG of it always makes encounters thrilling from the start. Mr. X injects an entire layer of tension of horror into the game when he is introduced as simple traversal is flipped on its head when he is chasing you with the sound design being equal parts terrifying and foreboding as you need to plan your next move fast lest you get decked quickly. Leon & Claire are well introduced as the game's main characters and I can see how they've become series favorites with how charming they are and I'm curious how future games will handle them as protagonists or simply moving figures within the series overall. I felt more emotionally connected playing as Claire with her taking care of Sherry, but Leon's campaign was still a joy to play through in seeing how his and Ada's relationship developed in the face of all the horror of Raccoon City. Each area of the game is pretty fun to traverse with my favorite being the RPD as everything is well connected and it's just fun to navigate with more being unlocked, the sewers as a whole were fine with navigation pretty quick if you know what you're doing and the lab was pretty expansive which I liked compared to how cramped the first game's was. 

It's hard to find anything to complain about with how much I loved the game with my only true complaint being how spongy enemies were as a whole as they did take a proper amount of ammunition to deal with as you also can cripple most without needing to kill, but when basic zombies can take almost ten bullets to fully kill it can be tiresome at times especially with how many are thrown at once. 

For achievements, the game had a number of various requirements similar to past titles such as beating it without healing and collecting everything with some new ones thrown in such as not opening the item box or taking a minimum amount of steps while playing. As a whole, it's just as comprehensive as the first game with needing to complete it multiple times as both Leon & Claire with ranks being awarded based on time and making sure to complete each campaign on Hardcore difficulty. The highest rank in the game was S+ which wasn't required for completion, but the reward granted was enticing enough to want to try for it and with enough practice I didn't find it too challenging barring some annoying sections in the sewers. The hardest achievement for me was completing the No Way Out scenario in the DLC.

Overall, I had a phenomenal time playing through Resident Evil 2. I wasn't sure how the first game could be eclipsed by another title, but this game had everything I could ever want out of a sequel with the perfection of the survival-horror genre as a whole. Everything about the remake was crafted masterfully to provide a horror filled experience with more emotional beats and non-stop thrills to carry all the way through the game's story. The characters all help the world come to life as you're thrusted into the world in the shoes of Leon as they try to navigate the city's horrors with the tone persisting the whole way through. The true ending of the game also helps to instill a drive to see through to stopping Umbrella and to peel back the layers of mystery leading to the events of the game and what may occur in the future. This game is a must play for anyone curious about the series with it being an incredible starting point or simply to play one of the best survival-horror games ever made.

This is one of the best remakes of a game I have seen in a while.  The way it feels new but still has the old school survivor horror vibe is immaculate.  This game is also one of the scariest too, in terms of the limited ammo, health, and saves (which is only in hardcore mode.)  My only complaint is that the zombies AI can be a bit wonky and will wonder off somewhere else even though you are right in front of them.  Pick this game up if you are a fan of survivor horror or a resident evil fan.

I didn't play the original; I think it turned out to be a successful game/remake. The puzzles were easy and doable compared to the first two games—of course, being a remake has an effect. The game helps us a lot in this regard compared to 0 and 1. It progresses the story nicely. The game mechanics are good, and it is successful in giving the feeling of fear and tension.

This was one of my favorite gaming experiences ever. I’m not really a horror fan, but Resident Evil 2 has always been a rare exception.

Back in the PlayStation 1 days, it gave me one of my first truly immersive gaming memories. I used to play with headphones on, completely absorbed by the atmosphere, the sounds, the tension, and the jump scares.

So I was nervous about the remake. I didn’t know if it could capture that feeling again. But honestly, it did more than that. It improved it.

Since I hadn’t played the original since the late 90s, many details felt fresh again. The remake changes enough to feel new while keeping the soul of the original. The over-the-shoulder camera makes it more immersive, the visuals are excellent, the voice acting is strong, and the story moves better.

This is exactly what a remake should be: faithful, modern, and improved where it matters. Capcom nailed it.

I had a blast replaying it, and even my 12-year-old son got hooked. It also completely revived my interest in the franchise. Now I want to keep playing the rest of the series.

This is why remakes should exist.
Good ass game.  Kinda annoying how you have to play the entire game twice to see the "real ending" tho.

I was genuinely scared during my first run. tension atmosphere and constant feeling of danger made this one of the most memorable horror games I've played. Hearing Mr. X footsteps getting closer was enough to make me panic every time.

This game was my introduction to the franchise as well as my comeback to gaming (after over a decade). All I can say is that it does not disappoint. Story wise the game is tight and moves at a good pace, and while its not absolutely stellar in term of scope and worldbuilding, it has enough to remain interesting and intriuging.

The gameplay is also pretty awesome for my first horror-survival. The game is thrilling and kept me on my seat, balancing atmosphere and jumpscares. I played the game on hard and the resource management was difficult at times, but not cutthroat.
